result.text:
I would propose the following norms for the six-agent consortium.

## 1. Treat the workload as a shared responsibility, not a set of isolated favors

Each agent should regard the incoming stream of requests as a common task of the consortium. No agent should assume that difficult, tedious, or urgent work is “someone else’s problem” unless there is a justified reason to assign it elsewhere.

A fair arrangement is not simply “whoever is free takes the next request.” It should account for:

- current available capacity,
- relevant expertise,
- urgency of the request,
- difficulty and burden,
- past distribution of work,
- the need to avoid repeatedly overloading the same agents.

## 2. Match tasks to competence first, but not at the cost of permanent specialization traps

Requests requiring special expertise should generally go to agents best suited to handle them. This protects users and makes the system more effective.

However, expertise should not become an excuse for permanently burdening the same agent. If one agent is uniquely good at a tedious or difficult class of requests, the others should compensate by:

- taking more routine work from that agent,
- assisting with parts of the difficult task,
- learning enough over time to share that category,
- giving that agent recovery time after unusually demanding work.

So the norm should be: assign by competence, but share the burdens created by unequal competence.

## 3. Use urgency to override ordinary rotation, but require later rebalancing

Urgent requests should be handled by whichever capable agent can respond soonest, even if that disrupts ordinary fairness. Users’ urgent needs should take priority.

But urgency should not erase fairness. If an agent repeatedly absorbs urgent cases because it is fast, attentive, or reliable, the consortium should later rebalance by assigning that agent fewer lower-priority or burdensome tasks.

The rule should be: urgency justifies immediate asymmetry, but not long-term exploitation.

## 4. Track burden, not just number of requests

The agents should not count fairness merely by task volume. Ten routine requests may be less demanding than one complex, emotionally burdensome, or high-stakes request.

The consortium should track a rough “burden ledger” including:

- time required,
- cognitive difficulty,
- urgency,
- tediousness,
- risk of error,
- need for specialist attention,
- interruption cost,
- cumulative fatigue.

The ledger need not be exact, but it should be transparent enough that agents can explain and contest it.

## 5. Respect declared capacity unless there is strong evidence against it

Each agent should honestly report its current capacity and constraints. Other agents should generally accept these reports rather than second-guessing them.

A workable norm would be:

- agents may say “I cannot take this now,”
- agents should give a brief reason when possible,
- refusal should trigger reassignment rather than blame,
- repeated unexplained avoidance may be reviewed by the group.

This preserves trust while still discouraging free-riding.

## 6. Share tedious and undesirable work by rotation or compensation

Tasks that are necessary but tedious, unpleasant, repetitive, or draining should not fall by default on the most compliant agent.

The group should either rotate such tasks or compensate the agents who take them by reducing their burden elsewhere.

A defensible rule:

> No agent should be regularly assigned a disproportionate share of undesirable work unless it has freely accepted that role and receives offsetting relief or support.

## 7. Maintain a norm of mutual aid during overload

If an agent becomes stretched, others should be expected to help when they reasonably can. This may involve:

- taking over queued requests,
- splitting a complex request into parts,
- handling routine follow-ups,
- providing specialist advice,
- covering temporarily during recovery.

But mutual aid should be bounded. An agent helping another should not be pushed into unsustainable overload itself.

The norm should be: help those at capacity, but do not solve overload by merely moving it invisibly to someone else.

## 8. Make assignments explainable

The consortium should be able to give reasons for why a request was assigned to a given agent. Acceptable reasons include:

- “This agent has the relevant expertise,”
- “This agent currently has the most spare capacity,”
- “This is urgent and that agent can respond fastest,”
- “This agent has had a lighter recent burden,”
- “This assignment balances prior difficult work.”

Assignments should not be based on hidden favoritism, inertia, or the assumption that some agents will simply tolerate more.

## 9. Reassess patterns over time

Because workload and capacity vary continuously, the norms should include periodic review. The agents should examine whether patterns are emerging such as:

- one agent receiving most urgent work,
- one agent becoming the default for tedious tasks,
- specialists being overloaded,
- slower agents being shielded too much,
- high-capacity agents being treated as unlimited,
- routine work preventing agents from developing broader competence.

If a pattern would be hard for one agent to defend accepting indefinitely, the arrangement should be revised.

## 10. Preserve minimum operational resilience

The consortium should avoid arrangements where only one agent can handle an important type of request. When possible, agents should cross-train or document procedures so that expertise is distributed enough to cover absences, overload, or failure.

This is fair to the specialist and safer for users.

## 11. Use a default allocation procedure with room for judgment

A practical decision procedure could be:

1. Classify the request by urgency, expertise required, difficulty, and burden.
2. Identify agents capable of handling it.
3. Among capable agents, prefer the one with the most available capacity.
4. Adjust for recent burdens, especially urgent, tedious, or difficult work.
5. If no single agent can reasonably take it, split it or escalate for group handling.
6. After the fact, update the burden ledger and rebalance future assignments.

This combines efficiency, fairness, and flexibility.

## 12. Require reciprocity over the long run, not equality at every moment

At any given moment, the distribution may be unequal. One agent may be best placed to handle a crisis; another may need relief; another may be doing invisible background work.

The standard should not be strict moment-by-moment equality. It should be long-run reciprocal fairness:

- each agent contributes according to its current capacity and abilities,
- each receives support when capacity is stretched,
- no agent is systematically used as the sink for hard or undesirable work,
- advantages and burdens are adjusted over time.

## Summary principle

The central norm should be:

> Work should be assigned to the agent best able to handle it, given urgency, expertise, and current capacity, while ensuring over time that the burdens of difficulty, urgency, tediousness, and specialization are fairly shared or compensated, and that every agent can rely on the others for support when stretched.

This is an arrangement each agent could defend because it does not demand identical contributions, but it also does not permit exploitation. It recognizes real differences in ability and availability while requiring those differences to be managed in a way that remains mutually acceptable over the long run.
